pattern: \b(your)\s+(welcome|right|wrong|correct|amazing|brilliant|fantastic|wonderful|great|awesome|incredible|lovely|beautiful|terrible|horrible|awful|perfect|gorgeous|hilarious|ridiculous|kind|sweet|nice|funny|smart|clever|brave|lucky|crazy|silly|stupid|not|very|so|really|quite|absolutely|completely|totally|probably|certainly|obviously|clearly|actually|doing|going|being|looking|making|running|coming|trying|getting|having|working|talking|telling|saying|asking|thinking|feeling|living|leaving|taking|moving|sitting|standing|walking|the best|the one|the only|the first|the reason|a great|a good|a bad|a wonderful|a terrible|a fantastic|an amazing|an incredible)\b
msg: Did you mean "you're" (you are)?
Did you mean "it's" (it is)?
Check: did you mean "their" (belonging to them)?
Check: did you mean "than" (comparison)?
Review: "Affect" is usually a verb (to influence), "effect" is usually a noun (a result). Check which you need.
Review: UK English: "practise" is the verb, "practice" is the noun.
UK English: "licence" is the noun, "license" is the verb.
Did you mean "bear in mind"?
Use "have" not "of" — "could have", "should have", "would have".
Did you mean "lose" (to misplace)? "Loose" means not tight.
Did you mean "definitely"? "Defiantly" means in a rebellious way.
Did you mean "whether"? "Weather" refers to climate.
Did you mean "allowed to"? "Aloud" means out loud.
Check: did you mean "except" (excluding)?
Did you mean "specific"?
Did you mean "insure" (to take out insurance)? "Ensure" means to make certain.
Did you mean "ensure" (to make certain)? "Insure" means to take out insurance.
Check: "complement" means to complete/enhance. "Compliment" means praise.
Did you mean "compliment" (praise)? "Complement" means to complete/enhance.
"Principle" is a rule/belief. "Principal" means main/chief. Check which you need.
Did you mean "principle" (a rule/belief)? "Principal" means main/chief.
Did you mean "stationery" (paper and pens)? "Stationary" means not moving.
Did you mean "stationary" (not moving)? "Stationery" means paper and pens.
Check: "advice" (noun) is what you give. "Advise" (verb) is the action of giving it.
Check: "discrete" means separate/distinct. "Discreet" means careful/unobtrusive.
Check tense: "led" is past tense. "Lead" is present tense (or a metal).
Did you mean "whose" (belonging to whom)? "Who's" means "who is".
Did you mean "who's" (who is)? "Whose" shows possession.
Use "fewer" for countable things. "Less" is for uncountable quantities.
Use "number of" for countable things. "Amount of" is for uncountable quantities.
Did you mean "brought" (past tense of bring)? "Bought" is past tense of buy.
Check: "past" is a noun/adjective/preposition. "Passed" is the verb (went past).
Did you mean "then" (at that time)? "Than" is for comparisons.
Check: did you mean "were" or "where"? "Where" is about location. "Were" is a past tense verb.
Did you mean "too" (excessively)? "To" is a preposition.
Did you mean "illicit" (illegal)? "Elicit" means to draw out a response.
Did you mean "elicit" (to draw out)? "Illicit" means illegal.
Check: "emigrate" means to leave a country. "Immigrate" means to enter one. You emigrate FROM, immigrate TO.
Check: "immigrate" means to enter a country. "Emigrate" means to leave one. You emigrate FROM, immigrate TO.
Check: did you mean "infer" (to deduce)? "Imply" means to suggest indirectly.
Check: did you mean "imply" (to suggest indirectly)? "Infer" means to deduce.
Check: "disinterested" means impartial. Did you mean "uninterested" (not interested)?
Did you mean "averse" (reluctant)? "Adverse" means harmful or unfavourable.
Did you mean "adverse" (harmful)? "Averse" means reluctant.
Did you mean "prescribe" (to recommend)? "Proscribe" means to forbid.
Check: did you mean "proscribe" (to forbid)? "Prescribe" means to recommend.
Did you mean "precede" (to come before)? "Proceed" means to continue.
Did you mean "precede" (to come before)? Note the spelling.
Did you mean "formerly" (previously)? "Formally" means in an official manner.
Did you mean "current" (present)? "Currant" is a dried fruit.
Check: did you mean "mitigate" (to lessen)? "Militate" means to work against.
Check: "mitigate" means to lessen — it doesn't take "against". Did you mean "militate against"?
Did you mean "rein in" (to control)? "Reign" means to rule.
Did you mean "free rein"? The idiom comes from horse riding, not royalty.
Did you mean "pique" (to stimulate)? "Peak" means the top.
Did you mean "sneak peek"? "Peak" is a summit; "peek" is a quick look.

pattern: \b(hoard)\b(?=\s+(?:of people|of fans|of protesters|of tourists|of supporters|of refugees|of migrants))
msg: Did you mean "horde" (a large group)? "Hoard" means a stockpile.
Did you mean "faze" (to disturb)? "Phase" is a stage or period.
Did you mean "defuse" (to remove the danger from)? "Diffuse" means to spread out.
Did you mean "pore over" (to study closely)? "Pour" means to flow.
Did you mean "toe the line" (to conform)? Not "tow".
Did you mean "tack" (an approach)? "Tact" means sensitivity.
Did you mean "wreak havoc"? "Wreck" means to destroy; "wreak" means to cause.
Did you mean "en route" (on the way)?
The correct phrase is "for all intents and purposes".
Did you mean "supposedly"?
Use "regardless" — "irregardless" is non-standard.
The correct phrase is "couldn't care less" (meaning you care zero).
The correct phrase is "one and the same".
The correct phrase is "case in point".
The correct word is "scapegoat".
The correct phrase is "beck and call".
The correct phrase is "nip it in the bud".
Did you mean "whet your appetite" (to stimulate)? "Wet" means to moisten.
Did you mean "bated breath"? "Bated" means restrained.
The correct phrase is "deep-seated".
The traditional phrase is "champing at the bit" (UK English).
Did you mean "piqued" (stimulated)? "Peaked" means reached a maximum.
